evidence.symptom_phrase: "Allow getParameter to be executed locally against an arbiter in an authenticated replica set"

timeline.text:
"Allow getParameter to be executed locally against an arbiter in an authenticated replica set"

"""{panel:title=Issue Status as of Apr 29, 2015|borderColor=#ccc|titleBGColor=#6CB33F|bgColor=#EEEEEE}    *ISSUE SUMMARY*  Because arbiters do not replicate data, they do not have a copy of a replica set's credentials, and therefore refuse all authentication attempts when {{auth}} is required. As a result certain operations required for basic maintenance and for integration with MMS, that were previousily available via the localhost exception, are no longer possible.     This change makes following operations available to clients that have access to an arbiter via the localhost exception:     - {{getCmdLineOpts}}  - {{serverStatus}}  - {{getParameter}}  - shutdown    *WORKAROUNDS*    None.    *AFFECTED VERSIONS*    3.0.0, 3.0.1, and 3.0.2    *FIX VERSION*  The fix is included in the 3.0.3 production release.    {panel}    h6. Original description    In 2.6.x it was possible to execute getParameter against an arbiter in an authenticated replica set. In 3.0.1 it is not.    Please restore the ability to execute getParameter against an arbiter in an authenticated replica set, at least from localhost."""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Refactor how WiredTigerRecoveryUnit and WiredTigerSnapshotManager begin transactions at points in time"

timeline.text:
"Refactor how WiredTigerRecoveryUnit and WiredTigerSnapshotManager begin transactions at points in time"

"""There are [4 different Timestamp member variables|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/master/src/mongo/db/storage/wiredtiger/wiredtiger_recovery_unit.h#L151-L169] and equally as many booleans to track how we open transactions, timestamp transactions, and commit transactions. The relationship between the recovery unit and the snapshot manager is equally as confusing.     There are 5 ways to open a transaction and 2 ways to commit.    On top of that, the {{ignore_prepare}} flag has to be set in each of the 5 places we begin transactions, and sometimes with additional configuration parameters.    My suggestions for improvement (at a minimum) are:    * Only call begin_transaction in one place, potentially as a helper on the WiredTigerSession or the RecoveryUnit itself.  * Use an enum to keep track of what type of read/commit we are doing for the open transaction.  * To reduce timestamp confusion we should only have to keep track of 2 timestamps: when to read and when to commit.  * The SnapshotManager should only keep track of timestamps (or removed completely), and not be the designated caller to open special types of transactions."""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Dropping unsharded collection fails to synchronize with a concurrent shardCollection"

timeline.text:
"Dropping unsharded collection fails to synchronize with a concurrent shardCollection"

"""Currently the sharded collection drop code path branches for sharded or unsharded collections when in practice there is no reason why it should not be the same for both.    This causes race conditions between dropping an unsharded collection and sharding the same collection concurrently, because dropping the unsharded collection will not take the distributed lock.    Making both of them go through the catalog client also helps with moving that command to the config server primary."""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Cancelling heartbeats doesn't work after network operation scheduled"

timeline.text:
"Cancelling heartbeats doesn't work after network operation scheduled"

"""When cancelling a heartbeat after it has scheduled the network operation, cancel doesn't work correctly from the client perspective because the handle that is cancelled is not the network operation. This is due to how we schedule work which then schedules the network operation, which does the callback (and reschedules more the next heartbeat).    One idea to fix this would be to keep a CancellableHandle which gets replaced with the newly scheduled network event so that a client can cancel the logical operation at any time."""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Cancelling heartbeats doesn't work after network operation scheduled"

timeline.text: "After investigating this, it is unclear what this ticket is referring to. The network operation's handle is tracked [here|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/859dfb093328ae9129f18952df4f25b123977a38/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl_heartbeat.cpp#L108] and then cancelled when a user calls cancel. Following through the callbacks, it seems all of the handles are tracked and untracked correctly and all are done under the _topoMutex. I think [{{_cancelHeartbeats_inlock()}}|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/859dfb093328ae9129f18952df4f25b123977a38/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl_heartbeat.cpp#L622] is called without the _topoMutex being held [here|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/859dfb093328ae9129f18952df4f25b123977a38/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl.cpp#L3652] which could be a problem, but that's not what this ticket is referring to.    I'm putting this back on the backlog for future investigation."

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Cancelling heartbeats doesn't work after network operation scheduled"

timeline.text: "In both [_scheduleMemberHeartbeatToTarget|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/r3.5.4/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl_heartbeat.cpp#L125] and [_doMemberHeartbeat|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/r3.5.4/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl_heartbeat.cpp#L89], the handles returned by [scheduleWorkAt|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/r3.5.4/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl_heartbeat.cpp#L130] and [scheduleRemoteCommand|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/r3.5.4/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl_heartbeat.cpp#L122] are tracked by [_trackHeartbeatHandle|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/r3.5.4/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl_heartbeat.cpp#L634].     The behavior of [ReplicationExecutor::scheduleRemoteCommand|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/r3.5.4/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_executor.cpp#L328] is specified by the TaskExecutor interface as:  {code:cpp}      /**       * Schedules ""cb"" to be run by the executor with the result of executing the remote command       * described by ""request"".       *       * Returns a handle for waiting on or canceling the callback, or       * ErrorCodes::ShutdownInProgress.       *       * May be called by client threads or callbacks running in the executor.       */       virtual StatusWith<CallbackHandle> scheduleWorkAt(Date_t when, const CallbackFn& work) = 0;        We cancel heartbeats by calling [cancel|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/r3.5.4/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_executor.cpp#L258] on the handles returned by these functions in [_cancelHeartbeats_inlock|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/r3.5.4/src/mongo/db/repl/replication_coordinator_impl_heartbeat.cpp#L649]. If these cancel operations do not behave the way they are specified by the TaskExecutor interface, that should be marked as an issue with with the ReplicationExecutor implementation. The tracking of all heartbeat handles seems to be done correctly, however, at the ReplicationCoordinator layer.      "

timeline.text:
"downgrade_replset.js and minor_version_downgrade_replset.js can fail due to v:2 index"

"""These tests downgrade a replica set after running {{setFeatureCompatibiliityVersion:""""3.2""""}}. The sFCV command returns """"ok"""" after dropping a v:2 index on {{admin.system.version}} with """"majority"""" writeConcern. This means that after receiving """"ok"""", the index drop will not roll back, and all nodes will _eventually_ become downgrade-able (since a node can only be downgraded until after the v:2 index is dropped). However, not all nodes are immediately downgrade-able. The test can attempt to downgrade a secondary that has not yet applied the dropIndex command. The node fails to start up with an error such as the following:        In order to fix, the test should wait for the secondaries to <USER>up to the primary before attempting to downgrade.    If an operator encounters this situation in practice, the intention is that she or he restarts the node as 3.4 and tries to downgrade again once it has caught up."""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Composing Containerizer is not sending calls to still launching containers"

timeline.text:
"Composing Containerizer is not sending calls to still launching containers"

"""Currently the new composing containerizer it holds multiple containerizers and passes calls to the underlying containerizer that launched the container.  However, this introduces a new problem where the composing containerizer only forward update/destroy calls to the container once the container is finished launching, as it then internally updates the tracking structure which containerizer is a container launched with.  The symptom is that the containerizer that is launching a container won't get a destroy call and continue to launch the container, while the slave already removed the task and it became a orphaned container that is not tracked by scheduler."""

timeline.text:
"Circular call dependency between CatalogManager and CatalogCache"

"""The two implementations of CatalogManager::shardCollection (in CSRS and legacy) call ChunkManager::reload in order to refresh the cached collection metadata. This call however, internally accesses the catalog cache, which introduces circular dependency.    The CatalogManager should sit under the read-through CatalogCache so this dependency should not exist."""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Rename collection on a secondary can set the collection minimumVisibleSnapshot timestamp backwards in time after a background index build's

timeline.text:
"Rename collection on a secondary can set the collection minimumVisibleSnapshot timestamp backwards in time after a background index build's

"""AutoGetCollection [will throw|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/d989f45b6f9a802e3d4fe5c4cb34b5ddc28ed4eb/src/mongo/db/catalog_raii.cpp#L119-L126] if a caller tries to access the collection at a point-in-time earlier than the min visible timestamp. Because renameCollection sets the min visible timestamp back in time, callers can now access the collection at an earlier time than an index build completed. This can hit the [DEV (debug only) block in IndexCatalog::numIndexesReady|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/0f2b2a4654df2eaaa63d6b39d8320d0c68471998/src/mongo/db/catalog/index_catalog_impl.cpp#L975-L995] and invariant; or allow callers that expect an index build to be complete to find no ready index because we [filter out ready indexes based on commit timestamp|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/d989f45b6f9a802e3d4fe5c4cb34b5ddc28ed4eb/src/mongo/db/catalog/index_catalog.cpp#L69-L76], which can fail our testing that primaries and secondaries have the same number of indexes."""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Rename collection on a secondary can set the collection minimumVisibleSnapshot timestamp backwards in time after a background index build's

timeline.text: "I think if we made the setMinimumVisibleSnapshot() functions only ever increase the timestamp, i.e. skip setting any timestamps that are less than the current, then a lot of the test failures would likely stop. At least if dbhash's collStats cmd, and whatever else, retries on ErrorCodes::SnapshotUnavailable errors (what [AutoGetCollection appears to throw|https://github.com/datastore/mongo/blob/d989f45b6f9a802e3d4fe5c4cb34b5ddc28ed4eb/src/mongo/db/catalog_raii.cpp#L119-L126]).    I don't know what would happen if a user established an index using query, say, after or at the index commit timestamp X, but then a dropIndexes gets replicated that commits back in time at timestamp Y, where Y < X.    [~<USER>, [~<USER>, you both worked on / reviewed the ghost timestamping code, and so are more familiar with what's going on. Any ideas?"

evidence.symptom_phrase: "Heavy-duty GC makes the agent unresponsive"

timeline.text:
"Heavy-duty GC makes the agent unresponsive"

"""An agent is observed to performe heavy-duty GC every half an hour:   Each GC activity took 5+ minutes. During the period, the agent became unresponsive, the health check timed out, and no endpoint responded as well. When a disk-usage GC is trigged, around 300 pruning actors would be generated (https://github.com/apache/scheduler/blob/master/src/slave/gc.cpp#L229). My hypothesis is that these actors would used all of the worker threads, and some of them took a long time to finish (possibly due to many files to delete, or too many fs operations at once, etc)."""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "inverted conditional maans that FileAllocator::run fails to trap failed open()s"

timeline.text:
"inverted conditional maans that FileAllocator::run fails to trap failed open()s"

"""The code to allocate new data files reads as follows:                        try {                             log() << """"allocating new datafile """" << name << """", filling with zeroes..."""" << endl;                             long fd = open(name.c_str(), O_CREAT | O_RDWR | O_NOATIME, S_IRUSR | S_IWUSR);                             if ( fd <= 0 ) {                                 stringstream ss;                                 ss << """"couldn't open """" << name << ' ' << errnoWithDescription();                                 massert( 10439 ,  ss.str(), fd <= 0 );                             }   The sense of the conditional on the massert() is inverted. The block is only entered for an invalid file descriptor, hence within it fd is always <= 0. As a result, the massert() never actually triggers, and so the error is neither logged nor trapped at this point.  This isn't actually a big problem, as the invalid value in fd (typically -1) causes the system calls within ensureLength() to fail pretty soon after. (The posix_fallocate() fails and warns, and then the fallback code fails the first lseek(), logs and aborts).  Patches attached for master, and for 1.6.6  <USER>"""

evidence.symptom_phrase: "inverted conditional maans that FileAllocator::run fails to trap failed open()s"

timeline.text: "was fixed in march, 2011"
